reasoning:
The question asks whether a vaccine will have been developed for Acinetobacter infections, based on the Wikipedia article 'List of infectious diseases', by the specified resolution dates (ranging from late Dec 2025 to Mar 2026). As of December 2025, there is absolutely no clinical vaccine approved or available for Acinetobacter baumannii or any other Acinetobacter species. Searches for recent (2025) news show that multiple research groups are only at the stage of exploring mathematical models, early mRNA constructs, and pre-clinical "multi-epitope" vaccines in mice. Vaccine development has a timeline of many years to decades from pre-clinical studies to public availability. Given this timeline, it is medically impossible for a successful development and approval process to be completed by March 2026. 

The Wikipedia article 'List of infectious diseases' on Dec 13, 2025 shows a blank cell for "Teagan disease / Acinetobacter baumannii" vaccines. The resolution states the previous status was "No" on December 11. Changing this to "Yes" would require either monumental real-world news (which hasn't happened) or persistent vandalism that evades Wikipedia editors for the duration of the evaluation period. Since "Yes" requires an actual true statement for it to remain indefinitely, and Wikipedia is heavily moderated, the probability of it resolving to "Yes" on any of these upcoming dates is very close to zero. The tiny residual probabilities represent the chance of long-term unfixed vandalism or a bizarre unannounced approval somewhere leading to a Wikipedia edit. Uncertainty slightly increases with the time horizon as minor edits have more time to occasionally drift incorrectly.
